sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 65, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The bordered block can be rectangle or diamond (LEFT/RIGHT). Color is set by clicking top-right squares. SPACE stamps the bordered block's shape/color onto the lower blocks from the top. Rectangle stamps top 5 rows, diamond stamps a diagonal pattern. Goal is white 5×10 above purple 5×10 in the lower blocks. Currently lower blocks have a diagonal split.
- Open questions: Can I move the bordered block with UP/DOWN? Can I stamp on the bottom portion?
- Plan: Try moving the bordered block with UP/DOWN to see if it changes position, which could allow stamping at different heights.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 64 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The SPACE action with the bordered block at the bottom didn't stamp on the lower blocks. Instead, it seems to have added a purple column on the left side (col 24). Looking at the output:
```
33: pBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
34: pBBppppppppppBBB
35: pBBpppppppppWBBB
...
44: pBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
```
There's a purple 'p' at col 24 (the first character in each row). This is outside the lower blocks area (which is at cols 27-36). So the bordered block at the bottom position stamps on a different area (to the left of the lower blocks).
Wait, looking more carefully, the lower blocks are at cols 27-36, and the bordered block is at cols 18-24. So the bordered block is to the LEFT of the lower blocks. When I press SPACE, it stamps on the area where the bordered block is, not on the lower blocks.
This is a key insight! The bordered block stamps on the area where it is positioned, not on the lower blocks. So:
- When the bordered block is at the top position (rows 11-31), SPACE stamps on the top 5 rows of the lower blocks (rows 34-38).
- When the bordered block is at the bottom position (rows 33-44), SPACE stamps on... the area where the bordered block is (rows 33-44, cols 18-24).
Wait, but the bordered block at the bottom position is at cols 18-24, and the lower blocks are at cols 27-36. So the bordered block is to the LEFT of the lower blocks. When I press SPACE, it stamps on the bordered block's area, not on the lower blocks.
But the output shows a purple column at col 24, which is the right edge of the bordered block. So the bordered block stamped on its own area, adding a purple column on the right.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me look at the output more carefully:
```
33: pBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
34: pBBppppppppppBBB
35: pBBpppppppppWBBB
```
The first character 'p' is at col 24 (based on the slice `[24:40]`). The lower blocks start at col 27. So the purple column is at col 24, which is to the LEFT of the lower blocks.
Wait, but the bordered block is at cols 18-24. So the purple column is at the right edge of the bordered block. This suggests that the bordered block stamped on its own area, adding purple to the right edge.
Actually, I think the bordered block is overlapping with the lower blocks when at the bottom position. Let me check the ascii output more carefully.
Looking at the output, the lower blocks are at cols 27-36 (10 columns). The bordered block is at cols 18-24 (7 columns). So there's a gap of 2 columns (25-26) between the bordered block and the lower blocks.
But the output shows:
```
34: pBBppppppppppBBB
```
This is cols 24-39. Col 24 is 'p', cols 25-26 are 'BB', and cols 27-36 are 'pppppppppp'. So the lower blocks are at cols 27-36, and the bordered block is at cols 18-24 (with the right edge at col 24).
So the bordered block is to the LEFT of the lower blocks, and SPACE stamps on the bordered block's area, adding a purple column at col 24.
Wait, but the bordered block is already purple. So why does SPACE add a purple column at col 24? Maybe the bordered block's border is at col 24, and SPACE stamps the inner content onto the area to the right of the border.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just focus on the fact that:
1. When the bordered block is at the top position, SPACE stamps on the lower blocks (rows 34-38).
2. When the bordered block is at the bottom position, SPACE stamps on the bordered block's area (rows 33-44, cols 18-24).
So the bordered block's position determines where SPACE stamps. This means I can stamp on different areas by moving the bordered block.
Now, the key question is: can I move the bordered block to overlap with the bottom 5 rows of the lower blocks (rows 39-43)? If so, I could stamp purple on the bottom 5 rows.
Let me try moving the bordered block UP to see if it moves back to the top position.
Actually wait - looking at the image, the bordered block is now a RECTANGLE at the bottom. It changed from a diamond to a rectangle when I pressed DOWN. So DOWN not only moves the bordered block but also changes its shape.
Let me try pressing UP to see if it moves the bordered block back to the top and changes it back to a diamond.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Bordered block is at bottom as rectangle. Let me move it UP to see if it changes back to diamond

print("Moving bordered block UP...")
result = action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] > 40:
        print(f"Purple node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Bordered block area rows 20-35 ===")
for i in range(20, 36):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][14:32]}")

print("\n=== Lower blocks rows 33-44 ===")
for i in range(33, 45):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][24:40]}")

print(f"\nBoard changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
